questions.stem: A loop in a space $X$ based at a point $x_0$ is:
options: a continuous path $\gamma : [0,1] \to X$ with $\gamma(0) = \gamma(1) = x_0$.
questions.explanation: A loop based at $x_0$ is a path whose start and end both equal the basepoint: $\gamma(0) = \gamma(1) = x_0$. It is the image of the circle, or equivalently a path with coinciding endpoints.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ is defined as the set of:
options: path-homotopy classes of loops based at $x_0$, with the operation induced by concatenation.
all loops based at $x_0$, under concatenation.
questions.explanation: Elements of $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ are path-homotopy classes (rel endpoints) of loops at $x_0$. Passing to homotopy classes is what makes concatenation associative with inverses, turning the set into a group.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The group operation on $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ is given by $[\,f\,] \cdot [\,g\,] = [\,f * g\,]$, where $f * g$ is:
options: the loop that traverses $f$ then $g$, each at double speed.
questions.explanation: Concatenation $f * g$ runs along $f$ on $[0, 1/2]$ and along $g$ on $[1/2, 1]$, each reparametrized to double speed. This is well-defined on path-homotopy classes.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Why is concatenation a well-defined operation on path-homotopy classes of loops?
options: Because if $f \simeq f'$ and $g \simeq g'$ (rel endpoints), then $f * g \simeq f' * g'$ rel endpoints.
Because loops are determined by their images.
questions.explanation: The product $[\,f\,][\,g\,] = [\,f * g\,]$ is independent of representatives precisely because path homotopies of the factors combine into a path homotopy of the concatenation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The identity element of $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ is the class of:
options: the reverse of an arbitrary loop.
a loop generating the whole group.
the constant loop $c_{x_0}$ at the basepoint.
questions.explanation: The constant loop $c_{x_0}(s) = x_0$ is the identity: for any loop $f$, $c_{x_0} * f \simeq f \simeq f * c_{x_0}$ rel endpoints.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The inverse of a class $[\,f\,]$ in $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ is:
options: $[\,\bar{f}\,]$, the class of the reverse loop $\bar{f}(s) = f(1-s)$.
questions.explanation: The reverse loop $\bar{f}(s) = f(1-s)$ satisfies $f * \bar{f} \simeq c_{x_0} \simeq \bar{f} * f$ rel endpoints, so $[\,\bar{f}\,] = [\,f\,]^{-1}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which group axiom for $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ relies on the standard reparametrization homotopy showing $(f * g) * h \simeq f * (g * h)$?
questions.explanation: Concatenation is associative only up to path homotopy: $(f * g) * h$ and $f * (g * h)$ differ by a reparametrization of $[0,1]$, and they are path-homotopic. This gives associativity in $\pi_1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the fundamental group of an arbitrary space necessarily abelian?
options: Yes, but only for path-connected spaces.
No; $\pi_1$ can be nonabelian, for example $\pi_1$ of a figure eight.
questions.explanation: The fundamental group need not be abelian. The figure eight (wedge of two circles) has free fundamental group on two generators, which is nonabelian. Higher homotopy groups $\pi_n$ for $n \ge 2$ are always abelian, but $\pi_1$ is not.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Given a path $\alpha$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ in $X$, the map $\hat{\alpha} : \pi_1(X, x_0) \to \pi_1(X, x_1)$ defined by $\hat{\alpha}([\,f\,]) = [\,\bar{\alpha} * f * \alpha\,]$ is:
options: a surjection that is not injective.
questions.explanation: Conjugation by a path $\alpha$ gives a group isomorphism $\hat{\alpha} : \pi_1(X, x_0) \to \pi_1(X, x_1)$. Its inverse is $\widehat{\bar{\alpha}}$. This is the key fact relating fundamental groups at different basepoints.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $X$ is path-connected, then for any two basepoints $x_0, x_1$ the groups $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ and $\pi_1(X, x_1)$ are:
questions.explanation: When $X$ is path-connected there is a path between any two basepoints, so the change-of-basepoint isomorphism makes $\pi_1(X, x_0) \cong \pi_1(X, x_1)$. One then often writes $\pi_1(X)$ for the abstract isomorphism class.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The change-of-basepoint isomorphism $\hat{\alpha}$ depends on the path $\alpha$ only through:
options: nothing; it is always the identity.
questions.explanation: Path-homotopic paths $\alpha \simeq \alpha'$ induce the same isomorphism $\hat{\alpha} = \hat{\alpha}'$. Different homotopy classes of paths can induce isomorphisms differing by an inner automorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is called simply connected if it is:
options: path-connected and has trivial fundamental group.
questions.explanation: Simply connected means path-connected together with $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ trivial for one (hence every) basepoint. Intuitively every loop can be contracted to a point.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a simply connected space, any two paths with the same endpoints are:
questions.explanation: If $\pi_1$ is trivial and $X$ is path-connected, then any two paths $\alpha, \beta$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ satisfy $\alpha \simeq \beta$ rel endpoints, because $\alpha * \bar{\beta}$ is a null-homotopic loop.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A continuous map $h : X \to Y$ with $h(x_0) = y_0$ induces a map $h_* : \pi_1(X, x_0) \to \pi_1(Y, y_0)$ defined by $h_*([\,f\,]) = [\,h \circ f\,]$. This map is:
questions.explanation: The induced homomorphism $h_*$ sends the class of a loop $f$ to the class of $h \circ f$. It respects concatenation, so it is a group homomorphism. It need not be injective or surjective.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The induced homomorphism is functorial: for composable maps $X \xrightarrow{h} Y \xrightarrow{k} Z$ one has
questions.explanation: Functoriality gives $(k \circ h)_* = k_* \circ h_*$ and $(\mathrm{id}_X)_* = \mathrm{id}$. These properties make $\pi_1$ a functor from based spaces to groups.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $h : X \to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $h_* : \pi_1(X, x_0) \to \pi_1(Y, h(x_0))$ is:
questions.explanation: By functoriality, $(h^{-1})_*$ is a two-sided inverse to $h_*$, so a homeomorphism induces an isomorphism of fundamental groups. Hence $\pi_1$ is a topological invariant.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group is most precisely a functor from:
questions.explanation: Because the basepoint must be specified and preserved, $\pi_1$ is a functor from the category of pointed spaces (spaces with a chosen basepoint) to the category of groups.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Homotopic maps induce the same homomorphism in a suitable sense. If $h \simeq k$ rel $x_0$ (a based homotopy), then on $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ one has:
questions.explanation: A based homotopy from $h$ to $k$ yields, for each loop $f$, a path homotopy from $h \circ f$ to $k \circ f$ rel endpoints. Hence $h_* = k_*$ on $\pi_1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which statement about the fundamental group of a product space is correct?
questions.explanation: The fundamental group of a product is the product of the fundamental groups: $\pi_1(X \times Y) \cong \pi_1(X) \times \pi_1(Y)$, since a loop in the product is a pair of loops in the factors.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Two loops $f, g$ based at $x_0$ represent the same element of $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ precisely when:
options: $f \simeq g$ as a path homotopy rel the endpoint $x_0$.
questions.explanation: Equality in $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ means path homotopy rel endpoints: a homotopy fixing the basepoint $x_0$ throughout. Free homotopy (not fixing the basepoint) is a weaker, distinct relation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ can detect a basepoint's path component only in the sense that:
options: it only sees the path component of $X$ containing $x_0$.
it equals the number of components.
it sees loops in every component of $X$.
questions.explanation: Every loop at $x_0$ stays within the path component of $x_0$, so $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ depends only on that component. Information about other components is invisible to $\pi_1$ at $x_0$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X$ be path-connected with abelian fundamental group. Then the change-of-basepoint isomorphism $\hat{\alpha}$ between $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ and $\pi_1(X, x_1)$:
options: depends heavily on the choice of path $\alpha$.
is independent of the choice of path $\alpha$.
questions.explanation: Different paths give isomorphisms differing by an inner automorphism (conjugation). When $\pi_1$ is abelian, all inner automorphisms are trivial, so the change-of-basepoint isomorphism is canonical, independent of $\alpha$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A loop $f$ at $x_0$ is null-homotopic (represents the identity in $\pi_1$) if and only if:
options: $f$ extends to a continuous map of the disk $D^2 \to X$ with $f$ on the boundary circle.
questions.explanation: A based loop $S^1 \to X$ is null-homotopic exactly when it extends over the disk $D^2$ whose boundary is $S^1$. A null homotopy is precisely such a filling of the loop.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following is a correct reason that $\pi_1$ is a useful invariant for distinguishing spaces?
options: Homeomorphic spaces can have different fundamental groups.
If two path-connected spaces have non-isomorphic fundamental groups, they are not homeomorphic.
$\pi_1$ is the same for all spaces.
$\pi_1$ determines a space up to homeomorphism.
questions.explanation: Since homeomorphisms induce isomorphisms on $\pi_1$, non-isomorphic fundamental groups certify that path-connected spaces are not homeomorphic. The converse fails: equal $\pi_1$ does not imply homeomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Count the group axioms and identify the basepoint role for the fundamental group.
questions.solution: The set of path-homotopy classes of loops at $x_0$, under concatenation, satisfies the group axioms: associativity, identity, and inverses. The number of distinct two-sided identity elements in $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ is {{1}}, namely the class of the constant loop. The inverse of $[\,f\,]$ is the class of the {{2}} loop.
questions.explanation: A group has exactly one identity; here it is the constant-loop class, so the count is $1$. The inverse of $[\,f\,]$ is $[\,\bar{f}\,]$, the class of the reverse loop.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Decide basepoint dependence for a path-connected space.
questions.solution: Let $X$ be path-connected with basepoints $x_0$ and $x_1$. A path $\alpha$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ induces a map $\hat{\alpha} : \pi_1(X, x_0) \to \pi_1(X, x_1)$. Whether $\hat{\alpha}$ is a group isomorphism: answer {{1}}. The number of path components that a loop based at $x_0$ can visit is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Conjugation by a path is an isomorphism, so the answer is yes. A loop stays inside a single path component, so it visits exactly $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Characterize simple connectivity through the fundamental group.
questions.solution: A space is simply connected when it is path-connected and its fundamental group is the {{1}} group. For such a space, the number of path-homotopy classes (rel endpoints) of paths between two fixed points is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Simply connected means $\pi_1$ is the trivial group. Then any two paths with the same endpoints are path-homotopic, so there is exactly $1$ class.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Apply functoriality of the fundamental group to a homeomorphism.
questions.solution: A continuous based map $h$ induces a group homomorphism $h_*$ on fundamental groups, with $(k \circ h)_* = k_* \circ h_*$. If $h$ is a homeomorphism, then whether $h_*$ is an isomorphism: answer {{1}}. The number of generators needed for the fundamental group of a single circle is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: A homeomorphism induces an isomorphism on $\pi_1$ by functoriality, so yes. The circle has $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z}$, generated by a single element, so the count is $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
